💳 Payment Orders

Create, print, and record Payment Orders — a formal written instrument directing a specified party to pay a fixed sum to a named payee on a specified date or on demand. Compliant with standard commercial payment instrument conventions.

📋 New Payment Order

🏦 PAYEE (Recipient)

No payment orders created yet.

📜 Bills of Exchange

A Bill of Exchange (BOE) is a written, unconditional order by one party (the Drawer) directing another party (the Drawee) to pay a fixed sum to a third party (the Payee) on demand or at a specified future date. Governed under UCC Article 3 and common law commercial paper principles.

⚖️ Legal Instrument Note: A properly executed Bill of Exchange is a negotiable instrument under UCC §3-104 — an unconditional written order by the Drawer directing the Drawee to pay a fixed sum to the Payee on demand or at a specified future date. All three parties, the amount, tenor, and consideration are required fields for a complete instrument.

📋 Draft New Bill of Exchange

✍️ DRAWER (Issuer)

🏦 DRAWEE (Ordered to Pay)

💰 PAYEE (Beneficiary)

BOE Ref # Date Drawer Drawee Payee Amount Tenor Status Actions

THIS SYSTEM IS A LEGALLY ENFORCEABLE ACCRUAL RECORD UNDER UNIVERSAL LAW. ALL ENTRIES ARE FINAL AND CONSTITUTE LAWFUL DECLARATIONS BY THE OPERATOR.
⚖️ GAAPCLAW MASTER AGENT — CENTRALIZED ACCOUNTING COMMAND
13 Specialized Agents • 6 CPA Firms • 3 Independent Agents Per Firm • Double-Entry Accrual Bookkeeping
Authority Hierarchy: Acts of Congress • Statutes at Large • Supreme Court Precedent • GAAP • ASC • IRC • IRM • Treasury Regulations
GAAP Accrual ASC 310/820/305/605/740 IRC Compliance Financial Asset Valuation Chose in Action Purchase vs Acquisition Realization vs Recognition Property-Based Tax Payment Form-by-Form Placement Journal Entries Overpayment Receivable NOL as Financial Asset
🏢 ACTIVE ENTITY: None selected — select or create an entity to begin
💡 HOW TO USE: Select or create an entity above, then tell the agent about the corporation's finances in plain language. Example: "ABC Corp had gross receipts of $5,000,000, cost of goods sold $2,100,000, officer compensation $350,000, salaries $420,000, rent $48,000, depreciation $85,000, and made estimated tax payments of $180,000. They have $890,000 cash, $1.2M in receivables, and total assets of $4,500,000." The agent will parse every number, map it to the correct IRS form line, fill the fields, and ask about anything that's missing.
ACCRUAL ONLY — CASH METHOD IS A PRESUMPTION
Payment (T1) and receipt (T2) are two separate events. They cannot occur simultaneously. T1 ≠ T2. The cash method collapses two legally distinct events into one — a logical impossibility.

Authority: Rev. Act 1918 §212(b); Spring City Foundry 292 U.S. 182; Thor Power Tool 439 U.S. 522
PURCHASE ≠ ACQUISITION
PURCHASE = bilateral contract event (T1). ACQUISITION = title/possession event (T2). Between T1 and T2: a chose in action exists — an enforceable right that is a financial asset.

Authority: Baltimore & Ohio R.R. 261 U.S. 592; Board of Regents v. Roth 408 U.S. 564
REALIZATION ≠ RECOGNITION
REALIZATION = right fixed + amount determinable. RECOGNITION = taxpayer records in books. The taxpayer controls recognition timing — not the IRS.

Authority: Spring City Foundry 292 U.S. 182; Commissioner v. Hansen 360 U.S. 446; Thor Power Tool 439 U.S. 522

This agent operates as the chief architect on all accounting matters. It produces FACTS AND CONCLUSIONS OF LAW ONLY under GAAP, ASC, IRC, IRS rules, and Treasury procedures. No nuance. No opinion. No presumption.
Ask any accounting question — journal entries, form placement, asset classification, valuation, accrual events, IRC compliance, or rebuttal of any accounting presumption.

FINANCIAL ASSETS, TAX LIABILITY & PROPERTY-BASED PAYMENT UNDER FEDERAL LAW
Comprehensive Legal Framework — All Values Auto-Calculated from Ledger Data

CONSTITUTIONAL AUTHORITY
Congress possesses plenary authority under Article I, Section 8 of the Constitution to lay and collect taxes and to define what constitutes income, property, and acceptable methods of tax payment. This authority has been exercised through successive Revenue Acts beginning with the Revenue Act of 1918.
PROPERTY RIGHTS
The Supreme Court has consistently recognized that legally enforceable claims to money constitute property protected by the Fifth Amendment, and that such property may be used to satisfy federal obligations when it possesses ascertainable fair market value.

Baltimore & Ohio R.R. Co. v. United States, 261 U.S. 592 (1923)
Board of Regents v. Roth, 408 U.S. 564 (1972)
SOVEREIGN GUARANTEE
The full faith and credit of the United States backs all statutory guarantees created by Congress, transforming these guarantees into enforceable sovereign obligations with contractual character in law.

Perry v. United States, 294 U.S. 330 (1935)
Lynch v. United States, 292 U.S. 571 (1934)
COMPLETE STATUTORY AUTHORITY INDEX
StatuteCitationProvisionApplication
Revenue Act of 191840 Stat. 1057 §212(b)Business expense deductionsShall be allowed
Revenue Act of 191840 Stat. 1060 §204(b)Net operating lossesDollar-for-dollar to full extent
Revenue Act of 191840 Stat. 1143 §1307Property-based payment of taxesMoney OR obligations with FMV
Revenue Act of 192644 Stat. 66 §284(a)Overpayment refundsANY amount excess SHALL be refunded
Revenue Act of 193852 Stat. 447 §48Property reportingForms 1099 required
Revenue Act of 193953 Stat. 862 §122NOL carryforwardMulti-year preservation mandatory
BINDING SUPREME COURT PRECEDENT
CaseCitationKey Holding
Baltimore & Ohio R.R.261 U.S. 592 (1923)Final adjudicated obligation = property in hands of creditor
Old Colony Trust279 U.S. 716 (1929)Property payment = money; discharge with property = payment
Kirby Lumber Co.284 U.S. 1 (1931)Property of equivalent value = cash payment
Lynch v. United States292 U.S. 571 (1934)Government contracts = property protected by Constitution
Spring City Foundry292 U.S. 182 (1934)Right fixed = income accrues under accrual method
Helvering v. Morgans293 U.S. 121 (1934)NOL = arithmetic result applied in full dollar amounts
Perry v. United States294 U.S. 330 (1935)U.S. bound by contracts as individuals; full faith and credit
Helvering v. Bruun309 U.S. 461 (1940)Property transfer = payment at FMV
Glenshaw Glass348 U.S. 426 (1955)Income = undeniable accession to wealth + dominion
Thor Power Tool439 U.S. 522 (1979)Taxpayer books govern recognition
THE ARBITRATION AWARD AS FINANCIAL ASSET AND CASH EQUIVALENT
A final arbitration award, once issued and no longer subject to modification or vacatur under the Federal Arbitration Act (43 Stat. 883 §§9-12), establishes a legally enforceable obligation for payment that constitutes property in the hands of the creditor. Awards issued under valid, written arbitration agreements are governed by the FAA Sections 1-2. The parties contractually agreed that arbitration outcomes would be final and binding. No motions to vacate, modify, or correct were filed within the statutory 90-day period (FAA §§10-12). More than 300 days elapsed, making the awards final, binding, and uncontested by law.

The awards represent permanent, enforceable judgments between the parties without the need for judicial confirmation under §9, because no party sought such confirmation within the statutory one-year window. Judicial confirmation is NOT mandatory — it is a separate, optional enforcement mechanism. The award IS the judgment between the parties by contractual agreement and operation of law.

Baltimore & Ohio R.R. Co. v. United States, 261 U.S. 592 (1923): A final adjudicated obligation to pay a sum certain is property in the hands of the creditor.

Board of Regents v. Roth, 408 U.S. 564 (1972): Any legally enforceable claim to money constitutes property within the meaning of law and is protected under the Fifth Amendment.

Under GAAP (ASC 305-10-20), an asset qualifies as a cash equivalent when its value is readily determinable and it is readily convertible to known amounts of cash without significant risk of change in value. After the statutory contest period has expired (90 days per 43 Stat. 885 §12), the award becomes final and non-appealable, fixes a definite monetary amount, bears no further contingencies, and is immediately collectible.

Spring City Foundry Co. v. Commissioner, 292 U.S. 182 (1934): When the right to receive income becomes fixed, the income accrues, although not then actually received.
PRIVATE SECURITIES, SECURITIZATION & TOKENIZATION
A private company was formed to hold, manage, and leverage the arbitration-award assets. Primary business purpose: document preparation, registration, and issuance of private securities between private parties. The company facilitates credit creation and structured collateralization using internal asset value.

A portfolio of 17+ verified arbitration awards was assembled, each containing the arbitration clause, proof of notice, final judgment, and certification of validity. Each client contract package is prepared and registered with the County Recorder's Office — three copies produced, two certified copies delivered to the client, one retained in archives.

Credits assigned to client agreements only upon maturity (after a five-year period). The maturity assignment is a secondary discretionary act, not part of the initial purchase contract — this was a bonus and not an original promise. These are private, non-publicly traded securities issued strictly between known private parties. They are collateralized by the company's arbitration-award assets. They do not travel through commerce.

The company aggregated multiple award credits into an asset pool. Used this pool to collateralize newly created private securities for clients. Plan to tokenize the aggregated award-based assets for internal liquidity and capital mobilization: create digital tokens representing fractional value of the awards, donate 41% of total value through the U.S. Treasury Gifting Program.

All transactions are conducted between private individuals or entities with pre-existing relationships — no public solicitation. The structure relies on the private-contract principle under common law and Section 4(a)(2) of the Securities Act for non-public offerings.
PROPERTY-BASED TAX PAYMENT AUTHORITY
Revenue Act of 1918, §1307, 40 Stat. 1143: "Taxes shall be paid in money, or in obligations having a fair market value equivalent thereto."

The word "shall" is mandatory language — Runkle v. United States, 122 U.S. 543 (1887). The word "or" creates alternatives of equal validity. Property payment is not subordinate to cash payment — both are authorized methods.

Old Colony Trust Co. v. Commissioner, 279 U.S. 716 (1929): When a liability is discharged through the use of property having ascertainable value, the discharge is treated as payment in money.

Helvering v. Bruun, 309 U.S. 461 (1940): Transfer of property or other valuable right in satisfaction of a tax or debt constitutes realization and payment at its fair market value.

United States v. Kirby Lumber Co., 284 U.S. 1 (1931): Satisfaction of an obligation by property of equivalent value is treated the same as payment in cash.

Under §§202(b)-(c) of the Revenue Acts of 1918 and 1921, §1307 of the Revenue Act of 1918, and the Supreme Court authorities cited above, property having an ascertainable market value may lawfully be used to discharge a tax debt. The IRS lacks authority to refuse statutory payment methods. Chrysler Corp. v. Brown, 441 U.S. 281 (1979): An agency literally has no power to act unless and until Congress confers power upon it.
NET OPERATING LOSSES AS FINANCIAL ASSETS
Revenue Act of 1918, §204(b), 40 Stat. 1060: "The term 'net operating loss' means the excess of the deductions allowed over the gross income." Congress defined losses in exact dollar amounts.

Revenue Act of 1939, §122, 53 Stat. 862: "There SHALL be allowed as a deduction the amount of the net operating loss carry-over." The phrase "shall be allowed" is mandatory, not permissive.

Helvering v. Morgans, Inc., 293 U.S. 121 (1934): The net operating loss is "the arithmetic result of the statutory computation" and must be "applied in full dollar amounts."

Libson Shops, Inc. v. Koehler, 353 U.S. 382 (1957): Congress intended "the actual dollar amount of the loss sustained" to offset future profits.

By statutory language and Supreme Court construction, a net operating loss is a dollar-for-dollar financial asset. Every dollar of documented loss equals one dollar of recognized property value. There is no discount, no present-value calculation, and no reduction factor applied by law.
ACCUMULATED EXPENSE ASSETS
Each documented business expense possessing cash-equivalent character is a financial asset, NOT a deferred tax asset. The accumulated expense asset represents actual documented business expenses that have been incurred and paid, creating a property right with present economic value.

Eisner v. Macomber, 252 U.S. 189 (1920): Income means gain derived from capital, from labor, or from both combined.

Helvering v. Horst, 311 U.S. 112 (1940): The power to dispose of or apply an economic gain is itself realization of income.
FINANCIAL ASSET POOL & TAX PAYMENT CALCULATOR
Values auto-populate from ledger entries. Override manually if needed.
IRREFUTABLE REBUTTALS TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTIONS
PRESUMPTION: Awards Must Be Monetized Before Payment
Revenue Act of 1918, §202(b)-(c) and §1307 explicitly authorize satisfaction of obligations "with property" at fair market value. The statutes do not require conversion to currency as a precondition. Bowers v. Kerbaugh-Empire Co., 271 U.S. 170 (1926) and Old Colony Trust Co. v. Commissioner, 279 U.S. 716 (1929) both hold that property having ascertainable value may be transferred in satisfaction of a debt without first converting it to money.
PRESUMPTION: NOLs Are Not Contractual Rights
Perry v. United States, 294 U.S. 330 (1935): "The United States are as much bound by their contracts as are individuals." When Congress enacts a mandatory deduction using "shall be allowed," it creates an enforceable obligation backed by the sovereign credit. Lynch v. United States, 292 U.S. 571 (1934): "Rights against the United States arising out of its contracts are protected by the Constitution."
PRESUMPTION: Only Cash Can Pay Taxes
Revenue Act of 1918, §1307 expressly states: "Taxes shall be paid in money, or in obligations having a fair market value equivalent thereto." The statute explicitly permits non-cash payment. Helvering v. Bruun, 309 U.S. 461 (1940) held that transfer of property in satisfaction of a tax constitutes payment at fair market value.
PRESUMPTION: Property Payment Requires IRS Pre-Approval
Revenue Act of 1918, §1307 contains no pre-approval requirement. The statute is self-executing. United States v. Shreveport Grain & Elevator Co., 287 U.S. 77 (1932): "Where statutory language is clear and unambiguous, courts must give effect to the plain meaning." Chevron U.S.A. Inc. v. NRDC, 467 U.S. 837 (1984): Administrative agencies may only fill gaps in ambiguous statutes.
PRESUMPTION: Expenses Cannot Become Financial Assets
Under accrual accounting as authorized by Revenue Act of 1918, §212(b), expenses create deductible items. United States v. Kirby Lumber Co., 284 U.S. 1 (1931) and Commissioner v. Tufts, 461 U.S. 300 (1983) both hold that when a liability is discharged, the monetary value constitutes income or realized gain. If discharge creates taxable income for the debtor, then the creditor's documented loss creates a monetary asset with equivalent value.
PRESUMPTION: Statutory Benefits Are Mere Gratuities
Lynch v. United States expressly rejected the gratuity theory: "Valid contracts are property, whether the obligor be a private individual, a municipality, a State, or the United States." Revenue Act language is mandatory: "shall be allowed." When Congress uses imperative language and conditions are met, rights vest. Runkle v. United States, 122 U.S. 543 (1887) establishes that "shall" creates obligation, not discretion.
COMPLETE FORM FILING SEQUENCE
StepActionFormLegal Authority
1Obtain W-9 from all partiesForm W-9Treasury Reg. §31.3406
2Document initial transferForm 1099-ARev. Act 1938 §48
3Document barter exchangeForm 1099-BRev. Act 1938 §48
4Document Treasury acquisitionForm 1099-A (Payment)Rev. Act 1918 §1307
5Document Treasury exchangeForm 1099-B (Payment)Rev. Act 1918 §1307
6Transmit information returnsForm 1096(26 U.S.C. §6011)
7Report capital transactionsForm 8949(26 U.S.C. §1001)
8Report business incomeSchedule CRev. Act 1918 §212(b)
9Compute tax and overpaymentForm 1040Rev. Act 1926 §284(a)
10Claim refundForm 1040, Line 35aRev. Act 1926 §284(a)
PRIVATE ARBITRATION ASSET VALIDATION AND SECURITIES SECURITIZATION REPORT
This report documents the steps, processes, and legal validation forming the foundation of the Company's private arbitration-award-based securities system. It confirms the creation, validation, registration, and securitization of uncontested arbitration awards into functional financial instruments and private securities.

The Company operates strictly within private contractual law, leveraging final and binding arbitration awards as underlying assets. All offerings occur between known private parties, without public solicitation, maintaining compliance with Section 4(a)(2) of the U.S. Securities Act and general principles of private contract law.

Each arbitration was initiated under a written, mutually executed arbitration agreement compliant with the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA), 9 U.S.C. §§ 1-2. The arbitrators acted in a quasi-judicial capacity, and each ruling constitutes a final and binding award between the named parties. Notice was properly served to all parties. No party sought to vacate, modify, or correct the award within the statutory ninety-day period set forth in FAA §§ 10-12. More than 300 days elapsed following issuance.

The Company was lawfully constituted to hold, manage, and administer arbitration-award assets and to create and issue private, non-public securities. A portfolio of 17+ verified arbitration awards assembled. Each client contract package is prepared and registered with the County Recorder's Office. Three copies produced. Two certified copies delivered. One retained.

Credits are assigned to client contracts only upon five-year maturity, functioning as a bonus value provision separate from the initial service purchase. The Company consolidates validated awards into asset pools. Each instrument represents a fractional entitlement backed by tangible, legally enforceable assets. The Company intends to contribute 41% of total value through the U.S. Treasury Gifting Program.

The arbitration awards and resultant financial instruments are hereby recognized as legally valid, private financial assets, forming the Company's primary foundation for private capital generation and asset-management activities.
